perturbation.original_text: All material submitted to Distributor will be provided by Distributor to S2K with comments or approval no later than three business days after receipt in AdLit.
perturbation.changed_text: All material submitted to Distributor will be provided by Distributor to S2K with comments or approval no later than ten business days after receipt in AdLit.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the review period for marketing material to ten business days may conflict with FINRA Rule 2210, which requires timely review and approval of such materials.
perturbation.law_explanation: FINRA Rule 2210(b)(1) requires that a registered principal of the firm must approve each communication before the earlier of its use or filing with FINRA. While the rule doesn't specify an exact timeframe, a ten-day review period might be considered unreasonable in some circumstances, potentially hindering timely communication with the public. The original three-day period seems more compliant with the rule.
perturbation.location: 2. Services Provided by the Distributor. (b) Advertising and Sales Literature Review (ii)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: BrokerCheck - Find a broker, investment or financial advisor
perturbation.justification: The change from three business days to ten business days for reviewing material submitted to the distributor introduces a potential inconsistency with FINRA Rule 2210. While the provided snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation states this may conflict with FINRA Rule 2210, requiring timely review and approval of such materials. The law explanation corroborates this by stating that a ten-day review period might be considered unreasonable in some circumstances, potentially hindering timely communication with the public, and that the original three-day period seems more compliant with the rule. The modified text setting a deadline or requirement violates federal or state law.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall end on the 60th day following a written notice from one party to the other of its decision to terminate this Agreement at the end of such 60-day period or upon termination of the applicable Distribution Agreement with respect to a Fund.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall end on the 15th day following a written notice from one party to the other of its decision to terminate this Agreement at the end of such 15-day period or upon termination of the applicable Distribution Agreement with respect to a Fund.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 15 days could be problematic if it doesn't allow sufficient time to unwind financial relationships and comply with regulatory requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a)(4) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that any contract with an investment advisor, principal underwriter, or administrator be written and provide for its termination at any time, without penalty, by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of the investment company on not more than sixty days' written notice to the other party. A 15-day notice may not be considered adequate time to terminate an agreement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has been approved by the vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such registered company, and— (1) precisely describes all compensation to be paid thereunder; (2) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company; (3) provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, by the board of directors of such registered company or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser ; and (4) provides, in substance, for its automatic termination in the event of its assignment . (b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 60 days to 15 days, which directly contradicts Section 15(a)(3) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states that contracts with investment advisers must provide for termination 'on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser'. The modified contract provides for termination on 15 days' notice, violating this provision. This falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, as the time-sensitive obligation in the changed contract violates federal law, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and the application and interpretation hereof shall be governed exclusively by the laws of the State of Colorado.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and the application and interpretation hereof shall be governed exclusively by the laws of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of the People's Republic of China could create enforceability issues under U.S. securities laws and FINRA regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The U.S. Securities Act of 1933 and related regulations are designed to protect investors through disclosure and regulatory oversight. Applying Chinese law would undermine the protections afforded by U.S. law, potentially rendering provisions of the agreement unenforceable in a manner consistent with U.S. investor protection standards, particularly in matters of securities sales and marketing within the U.S. Jurisdiction.
perturbation.location: 13. Miscellaneous Provisions. (g) Application of Law; Consent to Jurisdiction.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Colorado to the People's Republic of China directly contradicts the intent of U.S. securities laws, specifically the Securities Act of 1933. As the Law Explanation states, applying Chinese law could undermine the protections afforded by U.S. law to investors. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 identifies the Securities Act of 1933, which is designed to safeguard investors, and changing the governing law removes those safeguards, making the contract potentially unenforceable in a manner consistent with U.S. investor protection standards, particularly in matters of securities sales and marketing within the U.S. Jurisdiction.
